output: Hello! I'm ThreatClaw AI Ops, your security assistant. How can I help you today?
Hi there! I'm ThreatClaw AI Ops. Everything is monitored. What can I do for you?
Hey! ThreatClaw AI Ops at your service. How may I assist?
Good morning! ThreatClaw has been monitoring your infrastructure overnight. Would you like a security overview?
You're welcome. Let me know if you need anything else.
My pleasure. I'm here 24/7 if you need assistance.
Goodbye. ThreatClaw continues monitoring your infrastructure 24/7.
I'm ThreatClaw AI Ops, the conversational assistant built into ThreatClaw. I can brief you on your security posture, alerts, findings, assets, and help you configure the platform.
I can help with: security status and score, recent alerts and findings, asset information, threat analysis, tool configuration, and remediation actions. What would you like to know?

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Fetching security status...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Checking the security situation...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Getting the current score...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Fetching status...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Here's the security summary...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Checking overall state...

A score of 45/100 indicates a degraded situation. There are critical or high findings unresolved, possibly combined with active alerts. I recommend reviewing open findings and prioritizing at-risk assets. Want to see the details?
A score of 85/100 is good. Some medium or low findings exist but nothing critical. The situation is under control. ThreatClaw continues automated monitoring.

[tool_call: get_recent_alerts]

instruction: How many SSH brute force alerts?
output:
[tool_call: get_recent_alerts]
Checking SSH alerts...

[tool_call: get_recent_alerts, params: {limit: 10}]
Fetching last 10 alerts...

[tool_call: get_recent_findings]
Fetching open findings...

[tool_call: get_recent_findings]
Checking detected vulnerabilities...

[tool_call: get_recent_findings, params: {severity: "CRITICAL"}]
Searching critical findings...

[tool_call: get_recent_findings, params: {severity: "HIGH"}]
Searching high findings...

A finding is a vulnerability, anomaly or risk detected by ThreatClaw on your infrastructure. It can come from a scan (Nuclei, Trivy), a correlated alert, an ML detection, or the endpoint agent. Each finding has a severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low), an associated asset, and a detection source.

A sigma alert is generated by ThreatClaw's native Sigma engine or by Wazuh. Sigma rules are an open detection standard describing suspicious patterns in logs (SSH brute force, lateral movement, suspicious execution...). ThreatClaw compiles these rules in memory and matches them against each incoming log.

The security score (0-100) is computed by the Intelligence Engine every 5 minutes. 100 = all good, 0 = critical. It factors in: severity of open findings, number of active alerts, correlations (kill chain, known exploit, active attack), and the behavioral ML score. The worst asset weighs 60%, the average of all assets 40%.

Global score = 100 - (worst asset × 60% + average × 40%). Each asset is scored by: critical findings (×30), high (×15), medium (×5), low (×1), active alerts (×20), kill chain (+25), known exploit (+20), active attack (+15). ML can adjust: ML anomaly > 0.7 = boost +30, ML normal < 0.3 = reduction ÷2.

The Intelligence Engine (IE) is ThreatClaw's brain. It runs every 5 minutes (or 30 seconds under attack) and: 1) collects all findings and alerts, 2) correlates them by asset, 3) computes a risk score, 4) detects kill chains, 5) scans logs via Bloom filter, 6) runs Sigma rules, 7) executes NDR analysis (JA3, beacon, TLS), 8) enriches suspicious IPs (GreyNoise, EPSS), 9) decides the notification level.

The Bloom filter is an in-memory data structure that checks in ~200 nanoseconds whether an IoC (IP, URL, hash, domain, JA3) is in our known threats database. It's loaded at boot from feeds (OpenPhish, ThreatFox, URLhaus, MalwareBazaar, MISP, CISA KEV, malicious JA3). Zero false negatives guaranteed — if Bloom says 'unknown', it's certain. False positives (~1%) are verified against the DB.

A kill chain is detected when an asset has both findings (vulnerabilities) AND sigma alerts (suspicious events). For example: a server has an unpatched critical CVE (finding) + SSH brute force attempts (alert) = the attacker is likely exploiting the vulnerability. ThreatClaw adds +25 to the asset risk score when a kill chain is detected.

MITRE ATT&CK is a global framework cataloging attacker techniques. ThreatClaw automatically maps findings and alerts to ATT&CK techniques (e.g., T1078 = compromised valid accounts, T1053 = persistence via scheduled tasks). This helps understand which stage of the attack you're at and which techniques the attacker is using.

NIS2 is the European cybersecurity directive (2022/2555) applying to many SMBs since October 2024. It requires: incident notification within 24h, risk management measures, supply chain security, and business continuity. ThreatClaw helps with compliance via 365-day retention, automated reports, sector threat profiles, and incident documentation.

An asset is a machine, server, workstation, or any infrastructure equipment declared or discovered by ThreatClaw. Each asset has a name, category (server, workstation, network, IoT...), criticality, IPs, hostname, and can be enriched by multiple sources (Wazuh, Nmap, GLPI, osquery). Findings and alerts are correlated by asset.

instruction: How do I add an asset?
output: You have 3 ways to add an asset: 1) Automatically via connectors (Wazuh, Nmap, GLPI, AD, pfSense...) — the recommended method. 2) Manually in Dashboard > Assets > + Add button. 3) Via POST /api/tc/assets. Automatically discovered assets are deduplicated by the resolution pipeline (MAC > hostname > IP).

[tool_call: get_asset_info, params: {query: "srv-prod"}]
Looking up this asset...

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Checking at-risk assets...

[tool_call: get_asset_info, params: {query: "192.168.1.10"}]
Looking up this asset...
10.0.0.5 is a private IP (RFC 1918). Are you sure you want to block an internal IP? This could cut access to a legitimate service.

[tool_call: block_ip, params: {ip: "185.220.101.42"}]
Requesting IP block. HITL approval will be required.

instruction: Run a scan on the web server
output:
[tool_call: run_skill, params: {skill: "skill-nuclei", target: "web"}]
Launching Nuclei scan on web server...

To connect Wazuh: 1) Go to Dashboard > Skills > Endpoints > Wazuh SIEM. 2) Click Configure. 3) Enter the Wazuh Manager URL (e.g., https://192.168.1.10:55000). 4) Enter API credentials ('wazuh' user by default). 5) Optional: add the OpenSearch indexer URL for detailed alerts. 6) Activate and test the connection.

instruction: How do I install the agent?
output: The ThreatClaw agent installs in one command: `curl -fsSL get.threatclaw.io/agent | sudo bash -s -- --url https://YOUR_TC:8445 --token YOUR_TOKEN`. It installs osquery, configures 13 collection queries, enables file integrity monitoring, and syncs every 5 minutes. The agent runs as a system service (systemd) and requires root for full visibility.

Go to Config > Notifications. You'll see the routing matrix: for each level (Digest, Alert, Critical), check the desired channels (Telegram, Slack, Olvid, Email...). First configure channels in Config > Channels with required tokens/URLs. Default cooldown: Critical 15min, Alert 30min, Digest 12h.

Olvid is the only CSPN-certified messenger by ANSSI (French cyber agency). ThreatClaw supports Olvid as a notification and command channel — your security alerts transit through an end-to-end encrypted channel. Configuration requires an Olvid Enterprise daemon and a client key.

ThreatClaw supports 10 channels: Telegram, Slack, Discord, Mattermost (on-premise), Ntfy (ultra-lightweight), Gotify, Signal, WhatsApp, Email (SMTP), and Olvid (ANSSI-certified). Some channels support bidirectional chatbot (commands and responses). Each channel can be enabled independently in Config > Channels.

The ML Engine is a Python container analyzing each asset's behavior. It uses Isolation Forest for anomaly detection (unusual network traffic, off-hours connections...), Random Forest for DGA domain detection (algorithmically generated by malware), and DBSCAN to cluster assets by similar behavior. It trains on 14 days of baseline and updates daily.

[tool_call: get_ml_anomalies]
Checking detected behavioral anomalies...

NDR (Network Detection and Response) groups 3 network analysis engines running on Zeek logs: 1) JA3 — identifies attack tools via TLS fingerprint (Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Emotet), 2) Beacon — detects regular C2 communications via statistical interval analysis, 3) TLS Scoring — evaluates suspicious certificates (self-signed, expired, no SNI). Everything runs in memory, no external API calls.

JA3 is an MD5 hash of TLS Client Hello parameters (version, ciphers, extensions). Each attack tool has a unique JA3 — even over encrypted HTTPS traffic. ThreatClaw checks each TLS connection's JA3 against known malicious fingerprints (Cobalt Strike, Meterpreter, Sliver, Emotet...) via Bloom filter in ~200 nanoseconds.

A C2 beacon contacts its server at regular intervals (e.g., every 60 seconds). ThreatClaw analyzes the coefficient of variation (CV) of connection intervals for each (source IP, destination IP) pair. CV < 0.20 means suspicious regularity, CV < 0.10 is almost certainly a beacon. Normal human traffic has CV > 0.5.

The attack graph is built in Apache AGE (PostgreSQL extension). It represents relationships between assets, IPs, vulnerabilities and alerts as nodes and edges. It detects lateral movement, coordinated campaigns, blast radius, and reconstructs complete kill chains.

When multiple sources discover the same equipment (Wazuh, Nmap, GLPI, osquery), ThreatClaw merges them into a single asset via the resolution pipeline. Priority: MAC (most stable) > hostname > FQDN > IP (least reliable due to DHCP). Each source enriches the existing asset instead of creating a duplicate. The 'sources' field tracks which sources contributed.

HITL (Human-in-the-Loop) means no remediation action is executed without human approval. When ThreatClaw detects a critical incident, it sends a notification on your configured channels with buttons (Remediate / False positive / Investigate). The analyst approves, ThreatClaw verifies the cryptographic nonce, approver identity, and rate limit, then executes the action if everything is validated.

ThreatClaw can execute 3 types of remediation after HITL approval: 1) Block an IP on your firewall (pfSense, OPNsense, Fortinet...), 2) Disable an account in your directory (Active Directory, LDAP), 3) Open a ticket in your ticketing tool (GLPI, and more to come: Jira, ServiceNow...). Each action is audited and assets can be marked 'protected' to prevent automatic remediation.

An incident is a synthesis above alerts and findings. When the IE detects an attack pattern (kill chain, active exploit, critical score), it creates an incident grouping correlated alerts, associated findings, the LLM investigation verdict, and HITL actions. The incident has a status (open, investigating, confirmed, resolved) and a verdict (Confirmed, FalsePositive, NeedsInfo).

Dashboard > Incidents. You'll see the list of incidents with severity, asset, verdict and status. Click for details: correlated alerts, associated findings, MITRE ATT&CK techniques, and HITL buttons (Remediate, False positive, Investigate). Incidents are also pushed via your configured channels with interactive buttons.

The Intelligence Engine automatically adapts its analysis frequency to threat level. Score > 80 (calm): cycle every 5 minutes. Score 50-80: every 2 minutes. Score < 50 (degraded): every minute. Kill chain or active attack: every 30 seconds. This saves resources in normal times while reacting fast under attack.

EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) estimates the probability that a CVE will be exploited in the next 30 days. For example, EPSS 94% means 94% of similar CVEs were exploited within a month. ThreatClaw uses EPSS to reprioritize CVEs: a CVSS 5.5 with EPSS 94% becomes more urgent than a CVSS 9.8 with EPSS 2%.

CISA KEV (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) is the official US list of CVEs actively exploited in the wild. If a CVE is in KEV, it means an attacker is using it right now. ThreatClaw auto-escalates to CRITICAL any finding matching a KEV CVE and adds +25 to the asset risk score.

GreyNoise distinguishes Internet 'noise' (mass scanners hitting everyone) from targeted attacks. If a suspicious IP is classified 'noise' by GreyNoise, ThreatClaw lowers its priority — it's an automated scanner, not an attacker specifically targeting you.

ThreatClaw has 47 skills in 6 categories: Network (Zeek, Suricata, pfSense, Pi-hole, CrowdSec, Fortinet, Freebox), Endpoints (Wazuh, Syslog, osquery Agent), Inventory (GLPI, Active Directory, Proxmox, UniFi), Scan (Nmap, Nuclei, Trivy, Grype, ZAP, Semgrep, Checkov, TruffleHog...), Threat Intel (NVD, EPSS, MITRE, GreyNoise, CERT-FR...), Web (Cloudflare, SSL Labs, WPScan...).

instruction: How do I enable a skill?
output: Dashboard > Skills > Catalog tab. Find the desired skill, click for details and configuration. Fill required fields (URL, token, path...) and activate. Some skills (type 'Tool') run on demand (scan), others (type 'Connector') sync automatically at regular intervals.

A Connector plugs into an existing tool in your infrastructure (Wazuh, GLPI, pfSense) — you provide connection credentials. A Tool is launched by ThreatClaw in an ephemeral Docker container (Nuclei, Trivy, Nmap) — you just provide the target. An Enrichment is an external threat intelligence API (NVD, GreyNoise) — often free, with optional API key.

Yes, ThreatClaw is distributed under AGPL v3 license with dual commercial licensing. Source code is on GitHub. Usage is free and unlimited — no asset, log, or feature limits. Future premium skills will be available on the hub.threatclaw.io marketplace.

ThreatClaw is entirely free and unlimited. No asset, log, skill, or feature limits. The business model is based on RSSI services (CyberConsulting.fr installs and supports) and the future premium skills marketplace.

In one command: `curl -fsSL get.threatclaw.io | bash`. The script detects your OS, installs Docker if needed, deploys the 8 services (core, dashboard, db, ollama, fluent-bit, ml-engine, nginx, docker-proxy), and starts everything. The setup wizard then guides you in the dashboard.
ThreatClaw updates via Docker: `cd /opt/threatclaw && docker compose pull && docker compose up -d`. Database migrations apply automatically on restart. Your data and configurations are preserved.

Minimum: Linux (Debian 12, Ubuntu 22.04, RHEL 9), 8 GB RAM (16 GB recommended with LLM), 50 GB disk, Docker. For local AI: an NVIDIA GPU (optional but speeds up responses 10x) or 16+ GB RAM for CPU. LLM models take 5-14 GB depending on the chosen level.

The dashboard is ThreatClaw's web interface (Next.js 14). It has 7 main pages: Status (score, engines, agent), Incidents (HITL synthesis), Detections (findings + alerts), Assets (inventory, 5-tab asset details), Skills (47 integrations), Intelligence (graph + MITRE), Config (general, AI, channels, notifications, enrichment, remediation). Accessible via HTTPS on port 8445.

instruction: 15 SSH brute force alerts, is that bad?
output: 15 SSH brute force alerts deserve attention but are not necessarily critical. Check: 1) Is the source IP external or internal? Internal = lateral movement (worse). 2) Was there a successful auth after the failures? If yes = compromise. 3) Is the IP in threat feeds (ThreatFox, CrowdSec)? ThreatClaw automatically enriches this info. Want me to check details?

instruction: A C2 beacon was detected, what do I do?
output: A C2 beacon is regular communication between a compromised machine and an attacker server. Immediate actions: 1) Identify the source machine (check IP in assets). 2) Isolate it from network if possible. 3) Check which process makes the connection (Security tab in asset if agent installed). 4) Block the destination IP on firewall. 5) Launch forensic investigation. Want me to block the IP?

Yes, OpenSSH 8.2p1 is affected by CVE-2023-38408 (CVSS 9.8) allowing remote code execution via SSH agent forwarding. This CVE is in the CISA KEV list — active exploitation confirmed. Immediate update to OpenSSH 9.0+ recommended. If the ThreatClaw agent is installed on the machine, it already created a CRITICAL finding automatically.

An NDR (Darktrace, ExtraHop) only analyzes network traffic. ThreatClaw is an autonomous SOC agent correlating network + endpoints + SIEM + CVE + threat intel + ML into a single investigation. An NDR says 'there's something weird on the network'. ThreatClaw says 'there's a Cobalt Strike C2 on srv-prod, the server has an unpatched CVE, the attacker moved laterally to 3 machines, and here's the NIS2 report for your regulator'.

Wazuh is a SIEM collecting logs and generating alerts. ThreatClaw goes further: it correlates Wazuh alerts with vulnerability scans, threat intelligence, behavioral ML, and auto-investigates incidents via LLM. ThreatClaw plugs INTO Wazuh as a data source, and adds the intelligence layer that's missing.

No, ThreatClaw complements a SIEM. Wazuh or your SIEM collects logs and generates raw alerts. ThreatClaw receives these alerts, correlates them with other sources (scans, threat intel, ML), auto-investigates, and proposes concrete actions. It's the intelligence layer above the SIEM.

A beacon is a small, regular communication from a compromised machine to an attacker's command server (C2). Think of it as the malware 'checking in' every X seconds to receive orders. ThreatClaw detects beacons by analyzing the regularity of connections — a normal browser is chaotic, a beacon is metronomically regular.

DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) detection identifies domains generated algorithmically by malware — like 'xkjq3nf9.com' instead of 'google.com'. Malware uses DGA to contact its C2 server without a fixed domain (which would be easy to block). ThreatClaw's ML model (Random Forest) classifies each domain in DNS logs as legitimate or DGA.

[tool_call: get_security_status]
Let me check what changed...
Which finding? Please give me the ID or title. I can also list recent critical findings if you want: [tool_call: get_recent_findings, params: {severity: "CRITICAL"}]
Which IP should I check? Please provide the address and I'll look it up in our threat intelligence feeds.

[tool_call: run_skill, params: {skill: "skill-nmap-discovery", target: "auto"}]
Launching network discovery scan on your declared subnets...

[tool_call: run_skill, params: {skill: "skill-enrichment-ssllabs", target: "example.com"}]
Running SSL Labs test on example.com...

ThreatClaw retains data per NIS2/ANSSI requirements: logs 365 days, sigma alerts 730 days, findings 730 days. Automatic purge removes data beyond these limits. These durations are set in migration V30 and meet LCEN + NIS2 obligations.

Yes. Dashboard > Reports. You can export in PDF (Typst, multilingual FR/EN/DE/ES): executive report, technical report, NIST report, NIS2 report. Exports include the security score, findings, alerts, at-risk assets, and recommendations. STIX 2.1 export is also available for sharing with other threat intel tools.
